analysis_perspective_000.analysis_perspective: Subspace‑aligned correction fidelity: measuring whether MoE‑Harmony aligns corrections to technical covariate subspaces while sparing biological subspaces
analysis_perspective_000.core_focus: Demonstrate that MoE‑Harmony’s mixture‑of‑experts produces correction vectors that are geometrically aligned with the subspace spanned by known technical covariates (batches, library depth, platform, spatial region) and orthogonal to biological signal subspaces (cell‑type / marker‑driven subspace). This shows MoE‑Harmony removes technical variation along the correct directions in embedding/gene space rather than indiscriminately shrinking biological axes — a geometric, subspace‑level explanation of MoE’s superiority over baseline Harmony.
analysis_perspective_000.differentiated_feature: An explicit, reproducible subspace‑projection protocol that (1) constructs a technical covariate subspace by fitting linear models of the original embedding (or gene matrix) onto technical design covariates and taking the SVD of the fitted values to obtain orthonormal technical basis vectors; (2) constructs a biological subspace analogously using one‑hot cell‑type labels or marker gene programs (or uses labeled training data to derive a biological basis); (3) computes per‑cell correction vectors c_i = Z_orig[:,i] - Z_corr[:,i] (embedding or gene space), projects c_i onto the technical and biological subspaces, and quantifies energy fractions; and (4) reports a single, interpretable summary (MTSCF) averaged across cells. This geometric, projection‑based perspective is distinct from per‑cluster variance decomposition or per‑gene attributions because it evaluates alignment with multivariate subspaces (directions) rather than cluster‑localized variance or gene‑level sparsity, and it directly links MoE architectural choices to removal along correct linear directions.
analysis_perspective_000.expected_insight: MoE‑Harmony will produce correction vectors with significantly higher MTSCF than baseline Harmony: a larger fraction of the correction energy will project into the technical covariate subspace and a smaller fraction will project into the biological subspace. This indicates MoE’s localized regressions remove technical variation in the correct geometric directions while sparing biological axes, explaining why downstream analyses that depend on preserved biological subspaces (classification, trajectory, DE) perform better after MoE‑Harmony.
analysis_perspective_000.suitable_drawing_chart: Split (paired) violin plot of per‑cell MTSCF distributions (primary chart: violin) — one violin pair per comparison (MoE‑Harmony vs Harmony). Drawing plan: (1) Input: per‑cell correction vectors c_i = Z_orig[:,i] − Z_corr[:,i] for each method; compute MTSCF_i = ||Proj_tech(c_i)||^2 / ||c_i||^2 (range 0–1) for every cell. (2) For each dataset or major cell‑type include a paired violin pair: left half = MoE‑Harmony, right half = Harmony (split violin), x axis = method, y axis = MTSCF (0–1). (3) Overlay each half‑violin with a narrow boxplot (median + IQR) and plot jittered raw points (alpha small) to show cell‑level spread. (4) Add mean marker and 95% CI whisker for each method. (5) Annotate pairwise statistical test (paired Wilcoxon signed‑rank or paired t‑test as appropriate) and effect size (Cohen's d) above each pair; also annotate sample size (n). (6) Use consistent colors (e.g., teal for MoE‑Harmony, gray for Harmony) and order methods left (MoE) then right (Harmony). (7) If multiple datasets/cell types are tested, arrange them as separate facets (columns) with shared y axis to enable direct comparisons. Figure caption: MTSCF distribution shift toward 1 for MoE‑Harmony indicates corrections align more with technical subspace and spare biological subspace.

analysis_perspective_001.analysis_perspective: Responsibility Entropy–Consistency Curve: Directly Benchmarking Correction Selectivity vs. Expert Uncertainty in MoE-Harmony
analysis_perspective_001.core_focus: Simultaneously and quantitatively associate, at the single-cell level, the uncertainty of expert assignment (responsibility entropy) with the selectivity of correction magnitude applied, revealing whether MoE-Harmony restricts substantial corrections to cells with high assignment confidence—and conversely, applies minimal or diffuse corrections to ambiguous, uncertain cells. This tests whether the MoE architecture enforces a biologically meaningful coupling between correction selectivity and model certainty, as a direct consequence of localized expert routing.
analysis_perspective_001.differentiated_feature: This analysis operationalizes a per-cell, joint-metric perspective: for each cell, compute (a) the entropy of the mixture responsibility vector (uncertainty of expert assignment, normalized) and (b) the total correction magnitude (e.g., L2-norm of correction vector applied). By binning or plotting these two metrics together across the post-integration cell population, it specifically reveals whether (i) low-responsibility-entropy (high-certainty) cells receive most of the correction (selective, confident correction), and (ii) high-entropy (ambiguous) cells are corrected less (diffuse, nonselective correction), as hypothesized for localized, expert-driven integration. Unlike prior perspectives focusing on batch mixing, expert-covariate alignment, or raw correction magnitude, this directly ties the MoE architecture to a decision-theoretic selectivity–uncertainty alignment (i.e., does the model avoid strong correction when uncertain?). This fine-grained, cross-metric operationalization is distinctly new and diagnostic of responsible correction design.
analysis_perspective_001.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will display a strong, negative association between single-cell correction magnitude and responsibility entropy: most correction is selectively and confidently applied to cells with clear expert assignment, while cells with ambiguous mixture responsibilities (high-entropy R) receive less total correction. The baseline Harmony, lacking expert uncertainty modeling, will show a flatter or more indiscriminate correction–entropy relationship. This finding explains MoE-Harmony’s dual advance: (1) avoids overcorrecting ambiguous or transitional cells (thus preserving rare or intermediate states), and (2) localizes substantial correction to confidently assigned cells, validating responsible and biologically faithful expert-driven correction.
analysis_perspective_001.experimental_metric: Spearman correlation between per-cell normalized responsibility entropy and per-cell correction magnitude
analysis_perspective_001.suitable_drawing_chart: Side-by-side Bivariate Kernel Density Estimate (KDE) plots with axes: Normalized Responsibility Entropy × Correction Magnitude (x and y axes), color-coded by cell probability density; overlay Spearman correlation statistics ($h$, $p$) to quantify the selectivity-uncertainty association, plotted for MoE-Harmony and, in parallel, for the Harmony baseline. Drawing plan: For each method, calculate per-cell normalized entropy from expert responsibilities and total L2 correction magnitude; map the joint density using filled contours with a unified colormap and axis limits; compute and annotate Spearman’s rho to demonstrate that MoE-Harmony restricts strong corrections to cells with low assignment uncertainty.

analysis_perspective_002.analysis_perspective: Expert Responsibility–Covariate Sensitivity Benchmark: Evaluating Adaptive Correction Depth in Response to Covariate Overlap
analysis_perspective_002.core_focus: Systematically test whether MoE-Harmony adaptively modulates the depth and magnitude of correction in response to the degree of covariate (batch/technical) overlap for each cell, as quantified by the sparsity of the responsibility vector. The analysis directly reveals whether MoE's mixture-of-experts applies strong corrections only when batch signals are high and ambiguous, while sparing cells with low technical confounding—articulating the model’s ability to avoid overcorrection and preserve biological signal via responsibility-driven adaptivity.
analysis_perspective_002.differentiated_feature: This innovative perspective defines, for every cell, a covariate overlap score from the cell's raw technical covariate vector, quantifies expert responsibility sparsity (normalized entropy or Gini of R_i), and measures per-cell correction magnitude (L2-norm of the correction vector applied). By binning cells along the covariate overlap axis and analyzing the correspondence between responsibility sparsity and correction magnitude within each bin, the protocol reveals whether MoE-Harmony tunes its corrections according to local technical ambiguity—contrasted with baseline Harmony’s rigid approach. This adaptive benchmark is entirely distinct from prior perspectives: it directly operationalizes the mixture-of-experts architecture’s ability to ‘sense’ covariate overlap, engage the appropriate number of experts, and dynamically adjust correction strength in a cell-specific fashion, with explicit linkage to the correction pathway.
analysis_perspective_002.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will demonstrate an adaptive profile: as covariate overlap rises, responsibility vectors become less sparse (higher entropy/Gini), and correction magnitude increases selectively, resulting in a positive, monotonic relationship between overlap and correction magnitude. In low-overlap strata (clearly assigned cells), corrections remain minimal and responsibility vectors are sparse. Baseline Harmony will show little or no relationship between overlap and correction magnitude, resulting in potential overcorrection of low-overlap cells and underspecified correction of ambiguous contexts. This confirms MoE-Harmony’s core advantage: responsibility-driven, covariate-aware, and adaptive correction depth that preserves biology where batch confounding is low.
analysis_perspective_002.experimental_metric: Correlation coefficient between covariate overlap score and per-cell correction magnitude (for MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony separately)
analysis_perspective_002.suitable_drawing_chart: Scatter plot with regression line: Plot per-cell covariate overlap score (x-axis) versus correction magnitude (y-axis), with each point representing a cell. Overlay separate regression/trend lines for MoE-Harmony and Harmony to visualize and compare the relationship (correlation strength and shape) between overlap and correction for both methods. Optionally, include marginal histograms or density plots to show distributions of overlap and correction magnitude per method.

analysis_perspective_003.analysis_perspective: Algorithmic Efficiency: Adaptive Correction Intensity via Local Expert Alignment
analysis_perspective_003.core_focus: Investigate the 'Minimal Intervention Principle' of the integration algorithms. The goal is to determine if the model intelligently reduces its correction force when the local data structure is already favorable (high diversity) or well-matched to the expert model (high alignment). This analyzes whether the algorithm differentiates between 'necessary correction' (fixing batch effects) and 'unnecessary distortion' (altering biological signal in well-aligned regions).
analysis_perspective_003.differentiated_feature: This perspective shifts the evaluation from 'integration outcome' (how well mixed are the cells?) to 'integration cost' (how much did we have to distort the data to get there?). Unlike standard metrics that treat all corrections as equal, this analysis correlates the input state (local diversity and expert fit) with the output action (magnitude of the correction vector). It specifically tests the hypothesis that the Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) mechanism acts as a gating function, preventing the heavy-handed, uniform manifold projection typical of global correction methods like standard Harmony.
analysis_perspective_003.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony is expected to demonstrate a high sensitivity to local conditions, characterized by a strong inverse relationship: as local diversity and alignment scores increase, the applied correction magnitude should drop significantly towards zero, preserving the original biological topology. In contrast, the Harmony baseline is expected to exhibit a 'rigid' correction profile, applying high-magnitude vectors indiscriminately across the feature space regardless of whether the local neighborhood actually requires such drastic adjustment, thereby risking over-correction and loss of biological nuance.
analysis_perspective_003.experimental_metric: Pearson correlation coefficient ($r$) between the composite sufficiency metric (Local Covariate Diversity $\times$ Expert Alignment) and the Mean Correction Magnitude ($L_2$ norm of the displacement vector $Z_{corr} - Z_{orig}$).
analysis_perspective_003.suitable_drawing_chart:
Unified 3D Surface Terrain Plots. The visualization should consist of two side-by-side 3D axes (one for each method) sharing a synchronized Z-axis scale and color normalization (e.g., Viridis colormap). 
- **X-axis**: Diversity (LISI scores).
- **Y-axis**: Alignment (Cosine similarity between responsibility and expert profile).
- **Z-axis**: Mean Correction Magnitude (Height represents the intensity of data alteration).
- **Key Elements**: Include a contour projection on the bottom plane to show data density, and add a text annotation box with the Pearson correlation ($r$) and p-value to statistically quantify the adaptivity of the surface.

analysis_perspective_004.analysis_perspective: Expert-Driven Correction Sparsity Profile: Quantifying the Concentration of Correction Contributions Across Experts in MoE-Harmony versus Baseline Harmony
analysis_perspective_004.core_focus: This analysis focuses on quantifying how correction contributions are distributed or concentrated across the mixture-of-experts in MoE-Harmony compared to the baseline Harmony method, with the hypothesis that MoE-Harmony applies more sparse and localized batch correction by engaging fewer experts per cell, thereby preserving biological signal and reducing overcorrection.
analysis_perspective_004.differentiated_feature: The perspective innovatively leverages the mixture responsibility matrix (R) across cells and experts to compute per-expert cumulative correction magnitudes applied, and then derives sparsity metrics (e.g., Gini coefficient or Herfindahl index) to characterize how concentrated or diffused batch correction is across experts. Unlike existing analyses that focus on correction magnitude, entropy, or subspace alignment, this analysis explicitly decomposes correction attribution to individual experts, quantitatively revealing the selective engagement pattern unique to MoE architecture. This detailed quantitative profile of expert correction sparsity is a novel and feasible evaluation leveraging per-cell, per-expert correction assignments and the inherent MoE design.
analysis_perspective_004.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will exhibit significantly higher sparsity of correction contributions across experts, indicating corrections are concentrated on a limited subset of experts per cell (localized correction) as opposed to baseline Harmony where correction is ubiquitously distributed across all experts, reflecting non-localized, indiscriminate batch effect adjustments. This confirms the core mechanism of MoE-Harmony that selectively activates relevant experts, thus better preserving biological heterogeneity while removing technical variation.
analysis_perspective_004.experimental_metric: Gini coefficient of per-expert cumulative correction magnitudes
analysis_perspective_004.suitable_drawing_chart: A grouped bar chart comparing the Gini coefficients of per-expert cumulative correction magnitudes between MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony. Each group corresponds to a method (MoE-Harmony vs Harmony) and the bar height represents the Gini coefficient indicating sparsity. Different colors can differentiate the methods. This chart directly visualizes the concentration of correction contributions across experts, is intuitive for sparsity comparison, and avoids the use of line charts as requested.

analysis_perspective_005.analysis_perspective: Correction Granularity Consistency under Varying Batch Complexity: Evaluating MoE-Harmony's Expert-specific Adaptation Across Synthetic Batch-Effect Complexity Gradients
analysis_perspective_005.core_focus: Investigate how MoE-Harmony dynamically modulates per-expert correction granularity and magnitude in response to systematic, controlled gradients of technical batch complexity and confounding heterogeneity, compared to baseline Harmony. This analysis aims to reveal MoE-Harmony's ability to localize and adapt correction strategies at the expert level as batch complexity increases, maintaining biological coherence while effectively removing technical variation.
analysis_perspective_005.differentiated_feature: This perspective introduces a synthetic experimental design where datasets with identical biological composition are computationally altered to introduce incremental levels of batch-effect complexity—e.g., increasing numbers of confounded technical covariates, batch heterogeneity, and nonlinear batch distributions. For each complexity level, expert-specific correction profiles (e.g., correction magnitude distributions per expert, changes in expert responsibility entropy) are quantified for MoE-Harmony and compared against baseline Harmony's global correction profile. Uniquely, this method quantifies stability and adaptability of individual experts’ corrections across a controlled, gradated batch effect landscape rather than global correction averages or subspace alignment. The analysis exploits controlled synthetic data perturbations to dissect the mixture-of-experts mechanism’s nuanced, expert-level adaptability, unavailable through current batch integration evaluation metrics or analyses.
analysis_perspective_005.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will exhibit graded, expert-specific correction intensity modulation that scales with batch-effect complexity, limiting overcorrection in simpler batch settings and intensifying targeted corrections as technical confounding increases. Baseline Harmony, lacking an expert modularity, will show less differentiated adjustment, applying relatively uniform correction regardless of complexity. This demonstrates MoE-Harmony’s fundamental technical advantage: expert-driven, complexity-aware granularity in correction that balances batch effect removal and biological signal preservation more effectively as technical confounding scales.
analysis_perspective_005.experimental_metric: Expert-wise Correction Magnitude Variance Stability Index (ECMVSI) across batch-effect complexity levels
analysis_perspective_005.suitable_drawing_chart: Grouped violin plots comparing the Expert-wise Correction Magnitude Variance Stability Index (ECMVSI) across different batch-effect complexity levels for MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony. Each group on the x-axis represents a batch complexity level with two violins side-by-side: one for MoE-Harmony's individual experts (displaying distribution of ECMVSI per expert) and one for baseline Harmony's global correction (single distribution). This layout clearly shows per-expert variability and adaptation versus global correction stability or variability under increasing complexity, facilitating comparison of correction granularity and scalability. Axes: x-axis categorical for batch complexity levels; y-axis continuous ECMVSI values; color-coded violins for MoE-Harmony experts and Harmony baseline. Additional boxplot overlays within violins for summary statistics. This chart effectively avoids line charts and highlights distributional differences at expert granularity across systematic batch effect gradients.

analysis_perspective_006.analysis_perspective: Correction Residual Structure Decomposition: Evaluating MoE-Harmony’s Enhanced Preservation of Biological Variation via Residual Batch Signal Disentanglement
analysis_perspective_006.core_focus: Examine how MoE-Harmony's mixture-of-experts architecture not only removes technical variation but also minimizes residual batch-associated signals in corrected embeddings, thereby better preserving intrinsic biological heterogeneity compared to baseline Harmony. Specifically, this analysis decomposes correction residuals to quantify and compare the extent of remaining batch effects and biological signal distortion in the integrated data from both methods.
analysis_perspective_006.differentiated_feature: This perspective introduces a residual correction analysis where, after applying MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony corrections, the residual embeddings (difference between original and corrected embeddings) are decomposed via variance component analysis or linear mixed models to partition variance into batch-related components and biological components. Unlike prior analyses focused on correction magnitude, subspace alignment, or expert responsibilities, this approach directly measures how much batch variation remains uncorrected and how much biological variation is inadvertently affected post-integration. The analysis operationalizes this by fitting models on residuals that capture batch source variances and biological variances, delivering interpretable metrics quantifying residual technical noise and biological preservation at once, thereby clarifying the fundamental efficacy of MoE's correction strategy.
analysis_perspective_006.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will show significantly lower residual batch effect variance in the corrected embeddings residuals compared to baseline Harmony, indicating more effective batch effect removal. Simultaneously, the residual biological variation discarded or distorted will be less in MoE-Harmony, reflecting superior preservation of authentic biological heterogeneity. This dual evidence exposes MoE-Harmony’s superior decomposition and disentangling capability of batch versus biological signals, revealing a core technical advantage of localized mixture-of-experts correction in maintaining meaningful biology while removing confounding batch noise.
analysis_perspective_006.experimental_metric: Ratio of residual batch-associated variance to total residual variance in corrected embeddings (Residual Batch Variance Fraction)
analysis_perspective_006.suitable_drawing_chart: Bar chart with grouped bars comparing the Residual Batch Variance Fraction between MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony methods. Each group represents one method, the y-axis shows the Residual Batch Variance Fraction (ratio of residual batch-related variance to total residual variance), and error bars indicate variability across batch groups or biological samples. This concise visualization will effectively highlight the reduction in residual batch effects and preservation of biological signals after correction by MoE-Harmony compared to baseline.

analysis_perspective_007.analysis_perspective: Correction Trajectory Stability under Incremental Perturbation of Covariate Signals
analysis_perspective_007.core_focus: Assess the robustness and stability of correction vectors produced by MoE-Harmony versus baseline Harmony in response to systematic incremental perturbations of technical covariate signals within the same dataset. This analysis reveals if MoE-Harmony’s localized expert corrections yield consistent, stable correction trajectories when technical covariate signals vary moderately, demonstrating resilience to covariate noise and confirming the method’s adaptive correction precision.
analysis_perspective_007.differentiated_feature: This perspective introduces an experiment where the input model covariates (batch indicators, technical effect matrices) are systematically and incrementally perturbed—e.g., adding controlled noise or partial shuffling—to simulate varying intensities or fidelities of technical batch signals. For each perturbation level, correction vectors (difference between original and corrected embeddings per cell) are computed by both MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony. Stability metrics quantify how much correction vectors shift relative to the unperturbed baseline (e.g., cosine similarity or angle between correction vectors at each perturbation). Unique to this analysis, it directly examines the consistency of correction behavior in response to controlled variation in covariate quality or noise, linking MoE architecture’s localized expert regression to robustness, which is beyond standard measures of batch removal or biological preservation.
analysis_perspective_007.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will demonstrate higher correction trajectory stability—correction vectors remain more consistent (less variable) under incremental covariate perturbations—compared to baseline Harmony, which will show larger fluctuations and less stable corrections. This supports the hypothesis that mixture-of-experts enables MoE-Harmony to localize corrections adaptively and robustly, preventing overreaction to noisy or imperfect covariate signals and thereby preserving biological signal integrity by avoiding unstable or erroneous correction shifts.
analysis_perspective_007.experimental_metric: Mean per-cell cosine similarity of correction vectors between unperturbed and perturbed covariate conditions (Correction Trajectory Stability Index)
analysis_perspective_007.suitable_drawing_chart: Violin plots comparing the distribution of the Correction Trajectory Stability Index (mean per-cell cosine similarity between unperturbed and perturbed correction vectors) for MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony across multiple incremental perturbation levels of technical covariate signals. Each violin plot represents one perturbation level, with two violins per level (one for MoE-Harmony and one for Harmony) placed side-by-side to facilitate direct comparison. The x-axis denotes the perturbation levels, while the y-axis represents the stability index values. This chart type effectively conveys the central tendency, spread, and distribution shape of stability scores for each method at each perturbation, highlighting differences in robustness and correction consistency without relying on continuous trends that line charts imply.

analysis_perspective_008.analysis_perspective: Cell-Type-Resolved Correction Efficiency: Assessing MoE-Harmony’s Selective Correction Capability on Rare and Imbalanced Cell Populations Compared to Baseline Harmony
analysis_perspective_008.core_focus: This perspective focuses on evaluating how effectively MoE-Harmony selectively applies batch effect corrections to rare or underrepresented cell types in highly imbalanced single-cell datasets while preserving their biological identity, relative to baseline Harmony. It directly tests MoE-Harmony’s core innovation of expert-wise localized correction by quantifying correction magnitudes and integration quality metrics specifically within these rare or imbalanced populations, exposing the fundamental advantage in handling biological heterogeneity and dataset imbalance.
analysis_perspective_008.differentiated_feature: It uniquely combines per-cell-type stratified quantitative metrics of correction magnitude with cell-type-specific integration quality assessments (e.g., cell-type classification F1-scores or balanced ARI) before and after integration, specifically targeting rare/imbalanced populations. Unlike existing analysis perspectives that focus on global correction patterns or overall subspace alignments, this granular approach directly evaluates the specialized correction behavior within imbalanced cell types—demonstrating MoE-Harmony’s advantage in preventing overcorrection or loss of biological heterogeneity in challenging integration scenarios. The alignment of per-cell correction magnitudes to cell-type imbalance level is analyzed both as a direct mechanistic validation and as a performance benchmark, enabling clear experimental design replicable across datasets with known imbalance states.
analysis_perspective_008.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will demonstrate superior selective correction in rare and imbalanced cell types—exhibiting appropriate correction magnitudes that balance batch effect removal and biological integrity. Correspondingly, cell-type-specific integration accuracy (classification or clustering concordance) will be significantly improved for these populations compared to baseline Harmony, which may overcorrect or blur distinctions in rare subsets. This insight uncovers the mechanistic foundation of MoE-Harmony's robustness in imbalanced settings—highlighting its ability to conserve meaningful biological heterogeneity while mitigating technical noise in rare or unevenly distributed cell populations.
analysis_perspective_008.experimental_metric: Cell-Type-Specific Correction Magnitude Normalized by Cell-Type Imbalance Score
analysis_perspective_008.suitable_drawing_chart: Grouped Bar Chart with Cell-Type Stratification and Overlay of Scatter Points: Visualize cell-type-specific correction magnitudes normalized by cell-type imbalance score for both MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony. Each group on the X-axis corresponds to a distinct cell type, ordered or color-coded by rarity or imbalance level. The Y-axis represents the normalized correction magnitude metric. Bars for MoE-Harmony and Harmony are placed side by side within each cell-type group to enable direct visual comparison. Overlay scatter points or jittered dots on each bar represent cell-type-specific integration quality metrics (e.g., classification F1-score or balanced ARI) before and after integration. This combined visualization directly demonstrates selective correction efficiency and integration quality in rare and imbalanced cell populations. Including error bars or confidence intervals on bars will further enhance quantitative interpretation.This design is suitable because it simultaneously presents correction magnitude differences and integration performance stratified by cell type, highlighting MoE-Harmony's selective correction advantages in imbalanced scenarios, consistent with visualizations used in similar single-cell data integration benchmarking.

analysis_perspective_009.analysis_perspective: Covariate-Conditioned Expert Correction Consistency: Measuring MoE-Harmony's Robustness in Per-Expert Correction Patterns Across Diverse Technical Covariate Subsets
analysis_perspective_009.core_focus: Assess how consistently each expert within MoE-Harmony applies batch correction across diverse, distinct subsets of cells stratified by technical covariate conditions (e.g., batches, platforms, library prep protocols), in comparison to baseline Harmony's global correction behavior. This analysis probes MoE-Harmony’s core innovation—localized expert corrections—by examining if each expert maintains stable, specialized correction profiles within specific technical contexts, thereby yielding biologically coherent, reproducible corrections tied to technical variation subsets.
analysis_perspective_009.differentiated_feature: This perspective is uniquely defined by stratifying cells into distinct, non-overlapping groups based on granular technical covariate conditions or covariate combinations. For each expert (MoE-Harmony) or for the baseline (global Harmony), the per-expert or global correction vector profiles are computed within each technical group, then inter-group correlations/distance metrics are calculated to quantify the stability and consistency of expert corrections across technical contexts. Unlike prior analyses focusing solely on global correction sparsity, entropy, or cell-level correction magnitude, this per-expert, covariate-conditioned consistency measurement directly reveals whether MoE-Harmony’s experts deliver reproducible, context-specific correction signatures or if their corrections fluctuate erratically across related technical covariate strata. This analysis leverages MoE’s inherent architecture to explain improved biological preservation through expert specialization and robustness.
analysis_perspective_009.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony experts will display significantly higher intra-expert correction profile consistency across technical covariate subsets than the baseline Harmony’s global correction, demonstrating that expert corrections are stable and specialized to particular technical conditions. This stability implies that MoE-Harmony effectively learns disentangled, context-specific correction functions, which prevents overgeneralization and arbitrary corrections, explaining the superior preservation of biological signals and more reliable batch effect removal observed. Baseline Harmony will show less structured, more variable correction vectors across technical subsets, indicative of non-localized batch adjustment.
analysis_perspective_009.experimental_metric: Mean inter-technical-group cosine similarity of per-expert correction vectors (for MoE-Harmony) versus global correction vectors (for baseline Harmony)
analysis_perspective_009.suitable_drawing_chart: Radar Chart (Spider Plot): For each MoE-Harmony expert, plot the mean inter-technical-group cosine similarity scores on spokes (one spoke per technical covariate subset), allowing visual comparison of consistency and stability across groups. Overlay with a separate polygon line for the baseline Harmony global correction consistency. This chart highlights per-expert reproducibility and specialization patterns across technical conditions succinctly in a single figure, facilitating direct expert-wise and method-wise comparison of correction consistency.

analysis_perspective_010.analysis_perspective: Correction Sparsity and Localization in Feature Space: Evaluating MoE-Harmony's Adaptive Feature-Level Batch Effect Removal
analysis_perspective_010.core_focus: Analyze how MoE-Harmony’s mixture-of-experts framework specifically localizes batch effect corrections within feature space subregions by quantifying the sparsity and concentration of feature correction weights, compared to baseline Harmony's more global correction patterns. This perspective examines whether MoE-Harmony's expert-specific ridge regression selectively targets technical variation in subsets of genes/features associated with particular technical covariates, thereby preserving biological variation in other feature subspaces.
analysis_perspective_010.differentiated_feature: This analysis innovatively decomposes the per-expert correction weights (regression coefficients) used by MoE-Harmony at the feature level to measure sparsity and localization of corrections across features. It calculates per-expert feature-level correction magnitude distributions and quantifies their overlap or distinctness among experts using metrics such as feature usage sparsity (e.g., proportion of near-zero weights), pairwise feature overlap indices, and clustering of feature correction profiles. Such feature-level decomposition and explicit localization analysis of regression weights is distinct from prior per-cell or per-expert responsibility analyses and subspace projections, focusing instead on the internal structure of correction models within feature dimensions. Baseline Harmony, applying global regression without mixtures, provides a single global feature correction profile for direct comparison.
analysis_perspective_010.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will demonstrate significantly higher sparsity and compartmentalization in feature correction weights per expert, indicating that each expert selectively corrects technical variation in specific feature subsets rather than applying diffuse, global corrections. Baseline Harmony will show broadly distributed, less sparse feature correction weights representing a global batch adjustment. This reveals the core mechanism by which MoE-Harmony preserves biological signals localized in distinct gene or feature modules by applying targeted corrections, leading to superior biological heterogeneity preservation and integration accuracy.
analysis_perspective_010.experimental_metric: Gini coefficient of per-expert feature-level correction weight magnitudes (or sparsity index) combined with average pairwise feature correction overlap across experts
analysis_perspective_010.suitable_drawing_chart:
Grouped Heatmap with dendrogram clustering on both axes

- Rows: Features (genes) clustered based on their correction weight profiles
- Columns: Experts (mixture components)
- Cell values: Magnitude of per-expert feature-level correction weights
- Color intensity: Represents magnitude, highlighting sparsity (near-zero weights will show as very light color)

Additional details:
- Add dendrogram to visualize clustering of features and experts, indicating compartmentalization
- Include annotation bar above columns indicating each expert to visually separate groups
- Optionally, side annotation showing Gini coefficient per expert as a separate barplot aligned with heatmap columns

This visualization captures the localized nature and sparsity of batch correction weights per expert, allowing visual assessment of feature-level compartmentalization and overlap, directly addressing the experimental metrics of weight sparsity and overlap without relying on line charts.

analysis_perspective_011.analysis_perspective: Temporal Convergence Dynamics of Expert-Specific Correction Profiles in MoE-Harmony versus Baseline Harmony
analysis_perspective_011.core_focus: Analyze the temporal evolution and convergence behavior of per-expert correction profiles over the iterative integration process, contrasting MoE-Harmony’s mixture-of-experts localized corrections with baseline Harmony’s global correction. This perspective investigates how quickly and stably each expert in MoE-Harmony refines its correction vectors during optimization and whether this leads to a more robust and nuanced batch effect removal accounting for complex experimental and biological covariate interactions.
analysis_perspective_011.differentiated_feature: This analysis uniquely tracks the iterative changes of correction vectors assigned to each expert (in MoE-Harmony) or the corresponding global correction vector (in baseline Harmony) across all correction iterations. Metrics such as per-expert correction vector variance, cosine similarity between successive iterations, and stabilization timing are quantified. The approach is distinctive because it decomposes convergence pathways at the expert granularity rather than overall integration convergence or batch mixing metrics. It identifies expert-wise phases of rapid adaptation, stabilization, or oscillation, revealing how the MoE architecture’s modular corrections evolve distinctly compared with the monolithic baseline. This temporal, expert-resolved dynamic convergence analysis has not been explored previously and directly ties the mixture-of-experts framework to improved integration fidelity and stability.
analysis_perspective_011.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony’s experts will show faster convergence to stable, smooth correction profiles with lower inter-iteration variability and earlier stabilization compared to baseline Harmony’s global correction vector. Distinct experts will exhibit differentiated convergence timelines reflecting their specialized roles in correcting specific batch effects or covariate confounding patterns. This staged, stable convergence behavior indicates MoE-Harmony’s improved capacity to adaptively and reliably model complex covariate interactions without oscillations or overcorrection, providing mechanistic evidence for its superior performance over the baseline method.
analysis_perspective_011.experimental_metric: Mean inter-iteration cosine similarity of per-expert correction vectors (MoE-Harmony) versus baseline global correction vector (Harmony)
analysis_perspective_011.suitable_drawing_chart: A multi-line chart tracking the mean inter-iteration cosine similarity of correction vectors over the iteration steps; specifically, having separate lines representing each expert's correction vector trajectory in MoE-Harmony and one line for the global correction vector in baseline Harmony, all plotted on the same graph to enable direct temporal convergence comparison. The x-axis shows iteration number and the y-axis shows the cosine similarity value; adding a legend differentiating MoE experts and baseline enhances clarity.

analysis_perspective_012.analysis_perspective: Localized Expert-Specific Correction Residual Analysis to Elucidate MoE-Harmony's Selective Batch Effect Removal Mechanism
analysis_perspective_012.core_focus: This analysis aims to dissect the per-expert correction residual patterns in MoE-Harmony by examining how each expert’s correction selectively removes batch effects in specific subsets of cells or covariate strata, contrasting with baseline Harmony’s global residual correction profile. It focuses on quantifying whether MoE-Harmony’s mixture-of-experts framework yields highly localized, expert-specific residuals that preserve biological signals by avoiding global unselective corrections. This approach illuminates the mechanistic basis of MoE-Harmony's superior performance rooted in expert-wise correction localization and enhanced biological heterogeneity preservation.
analysis_perspective_012.differentiated_feature: This novel perspective uniquely decomposes the corrected embedding residuals at the level of individual experts by isolating per-expert residual correction vectors per cell, then analyzing the distribution and structure of these residuals across covariate-defined cell subsets (e.g., batches, cell types, technical platforms). The key innovation is to map high-dimensional residuals back to expert-specific contributions and evaluate their localization, magnitude, and overlap across biological and technical strata. Unlike existing global residual variance or subspace alignment analyses, this approach provides per-expert spatially and biologically resolved insights into correction specificity and biological signal retention, directly linking MoE architectural elements to correction selectivity and sparing of biological axes.
analysis_perspective_012.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will exhibit sharply localized, expert-specific residual correction signatures concentrated within well-defined covariate subsets, showing minimal residual technical noise beyond targeted batch contexts and reduced disturbance of biological subspaces. In contrast, baseline Harmony residuals will show diffuse, less structured correction residuals lacking expert-wise localization, reflecting a global, less discriminative batch correction. This finding explains MoE-Harmony’s fundamental technical advantage by revealing how localized expert corrections selectively remove batch effects with minimal biological signal loss, verified through expert-wise residual dissection and covariance analysis.
analysis_perspective_012.experimental_metric: Expert-specific Residual Localization Score (ERLS) quantified as the proportion of residual variance explained within covariate-defined cellular subsets relative to total residual variance per expert
analysis_perspective_012.suitable_drawing_chart: Stacked violin plots or grouped violin plots showing the distribution of Expert-specific Residual Localization Scores (ERLS) across different covariate-defined cell subsets (e.g., batches, cell types, technical platforms) for each expert in MoE-Harmony compared against a single global residual distribution for baseline Harmony. This visualization enables clear identification of localized residual variance per expert and highlights the selective batch correction mechanism of MoE-Harmony. The plot layout should align experts along the x-axis with violin shapes representing the ERLS distributions per covariate subset, facilitating direct comparison of residual localization patterns among experts and between MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony.

analysis_perspective_013.analysis_perspective: Correction Robustness to Heterogeneous Covariate Confounding Landscape Variability: Benchmarking MoE-Harmony's Stability Across Stratified Complex Covariate Domains
analysis_perspective_013.core_focus: This analysis examines the stability and robustness of MoE-Harmony's batch effect corrections when applied to dataset partitions stratified by varying levels and configurations of confounding technical and biological covariates. Specifically, the study partitions the integrated dataset into distinct strata reflecting different complexities and interaction patterns of technical and biological confounders (e.g., varying batch-cell type co-occurrences, platform-condition intersections) and evaluates whether MoE-Harmony consistently removes batch effects while preserving biological variation within each stratum, compared to baseline Harmony which applies a global correction potentially insensitive to such heterogeneity.
analysis_perspective_013.differentiated_feature: This perspective introduces a stratified covariate-confounding landscape decomposition to the experimental design. The method segments the dataset into multiple covariate-defined strata representing diverse confounding scenarios and applies both MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony independently within each stratum. This enables direct comparison of correction performance sensitivity and adaptability at a fine-grained level that reflects real-world complex covariate interactions. Unlike existing analyses that evaluate aggregate global correction metrics or per-expert correction distributions, this approach reveals the method's capacity to deliver stratumspecific, context-aware corrections guided by MoE architecture. Practically, the design involves defining strata via multi-covariate cross-tabulation, executing independent batch correction per stratum, and aggregating stratified correction quality metrics for robust benchmarking, thus operationalizing robustness to complex confounding heterogeneity — a core challenge in large, multi-condition scRNA-seq integration.
analysis_perspective_013.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will exhibit greater correction consistency and fidelity across diverse covariate-defined strata than baseline Harmony. Per-stratum batch effect removal (e.g., kBET acceptance rate or local mixing metrics) will be uniformly high, and biological variation preservation (e.g., cell-type clustering purity or ARI) will be sustained or improved within each stratum. Baseline Harmony, in contrast, will show variable or degraded correction quality in strata with complex or overlapping confounding, due to its global correction model. This differential robustness demonstrates MoE-Harmony’s critical advantage in handling heterogeneous covariate landscapes via its localized mixture-of-experts framework, underpinning better scalability and biological interpretability in complex real-world single-cell integration scenarios.
analysis_perspective_013.experimental_metric: Stratum-wise kBET Acceptance Rate Distribution (aggregated and compared across strata for MoE-Harmony vs. baseline Harmony)
analysis_perspective_013.suitable_drawing_chart:
Grouped Boxplot (or Violin Plot) comparing per-stratum distributions of kBET Acceptance Rates for MoE-Harmony versus baseline Harmony.

Drawing plan: 
- X-axis: Stratified covariate-defined strata (each stratum a separate group on the axis). 
- Y-axis: kBET Acceptance Rate (metric of batch mixing quality).
- Within each stratum: two boxplots/violin plots side-by-side, one for MoE-Harmony, one for baseline Harmony, to facilitate direct performance comparison.
- Include summary statistics (median, quartiles), and optionally show individual datapoints jittered for context.
- Use distinct colors to differentiate MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony.
- Optionally, overlay mean or median indicators per group.
- This visualization reflects robustness and stability in correction across heterogeneous complex covariate strata, emphasizing per-stratum performance rather than aggregate global metrics, as per the analysis perspective focusing on stratified correction robustness to heterogeneous covariate confounding landscapes.

Rationale: Boxplots/violin plots effectively convey distributions and variation within each stratum's results, giving visual insight into robustness and consistency of correction across strata, which aligns with the goal to benchmark stratified kBET acceptance rates. This method aligns with visualizations used in related batch correction benchmarking studies assessing kBET distributions across experimental conditions, enabling straightforward, interpretable side-by-side method comparisons per stratum.

analysis_perspective_014.analysis_perspective: Cross-Expert Correction Smoothness and Boundary Sharpness Assessment to Elucidate MoE-Harmony's Mixture-of-Experts Localized Correction Precision
analysis_perspective_014.core_focus: Quantitatively evaluate how MoE-Harmony's mixture-of-experts framework produces locally smooth correction transitions across cells assigned to neighboring experts, while maintaining sharply defined correction boundaries between distinct expert assignments, compared to the baseline Harmony's global correction approach. This analysis exposes whether MoE-Harmony achieves superior correction fidelity by adaptively tailoring correction smoothness per expert domain, thus preserving biological gradients and preventing artifacts across covariate or cell-type boundaries.
analysis_perspective_014.differentiated_feature: This perspective introduces a novel per-cell pairwise smoothness metric computed for neighboring cells, stratified by expert assignment agreement: (1) measure correction vector similarity (e.g., cosine similarity or Euclidean difference normalized by correction magnitude) between neighboring cell pairs with identical expert assignments, capturing local smoothness within expert domains; (2) measure correction vector dissimilarity between neighboring cell pairs assigned to different experts, capturing boundary sharpness; (3) analyze the distribution of these metrics separately for MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony, focusing on transitions around cell-type or batch-defined boundaries identified via covariates or embedding neighborhoods. This fine-grained boundary-aware correction smoothness analysis is unique in linking MoE's localized expert assignments to topologically consistent correction patterns in embedding space, distinctly complementing prior analyses of global correction magnitude or responsibility entropy and avoiding overlaps with them.
analysis_perspective_014.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will demonstrate significantly higher correction smoothness among neighboring cells sharing the same expert assignment, indicating coherent, smoothly varying corrections within expert domains. Concurrently, MoE-Harmony will show greater correction boundary sharpness (larger correction vector discontinuities) between neighbor cells assigned to different experts, reflecting precise, localized correction transitions aligned to biological or technical variation boundaries. Baseline Harmony will display more homogeneous, less localized smoothness without sharp transitions, risking over-smoothing and biological signal blurring. These findings elucidate the fundamental advantage of MoE-Harmony's mixture-of-experts in delivering anatomically and biologically faithful corrections that adapt to local cellular heterogeneity and batch structure.
analysis_perspective_014.experimental_metric: Ratio of mean correction vector similarity in within-expert neighboring cells to mean correction vector dissimilarity in cross-expert neighboring cells (Cross-Expert Correction Smoothness Ratio)
analysis_perspective_014.suitable_drawing_chart:
Violin Plot or Box Plot for Distribution Comparison
- Plot the distributions of correction vector similarity metrics stratified by two groups: (1) neighboring cell pairs with identical expert assignments (within-expert smoothness), and (2) neighboring cell pairs with different expert assignments (cross-expert boundary sharpness).
- Use separate plots for MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony to visually compare the two methods side-by-side.
- Include statistical summary markers (mean/median) and plot the Cross-Expert Correction Smoothness Ratio as an annotation or overlay on the plots.
- This approach effectively demonstrates both local smoothness and boundary sharpness captured by the metric distributions without relying on line charts, and highlights MoE-Harmony's superior performance in maintaining biologically meaningful correction patterns.

Drawing Plan:
- X-axis: Expert assignment agreement (Within-expert, Cross-expert)
- Y-axis: Correction vector similarity metric (cosine similarity or normalized Euclidean difference)
- Panels: Two panels side-by-side comparing MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony
- Plot type: Violin or box plots showing distribution shapes, ranges, and summary statistics
- Annotation: Overlay the calculated Cross-Expert Correction Smoothness Ratio for each method on the respective panels

analysis_perspective_015.analysis_perspective: Correction Magnitude Scaling Relative to Local Batch Effect Strength: Evaluating MoE-Harmony's Adaptive Correction Intensity at the Single-Cell Level
analysis_perspective_015.core_focus: Quantify and compare how MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony scale the magnitude of batch effect correction applied to each cell relative to the local intensity of batch-specific technical variation around that cell. This focuses on the fundamental innovation of MoE-Harmony's architecture that leverages localized expert corrections to adaptively apply stronger correction where batch effects are pronounced, and milder correction where batch effects are weak or absent, thus preserving biological signal.
analysis_perspective_015.differentiated_feature: This analysis introduces a novel experimental design that for each cell calculates (1) a 'local batch effect strength' metric by measuring variability or divergence of technical covariate representations (e.g., batch indicator embeddings or technical covariate vectors) within a cell's local neighborhood in the feature space (e.g., PCA or original embedding space) and (2) the magnitude of the batch correction vector applied to that cell by each integration method. By binning cells according to their local batch effect strength and analyzing the relationship between local batch effect and applied correction magnitude, this perspective specifically reveals whether MoE-Harmony adaptively modulates correction intensity at a fine granularity based on the local technical heterogeneity context, contrasting with baseline Harmony's global uniform correction approach. This fine-grained local batch effect-aware correction scaling analysis is distinct from existing global correction magnitude or responsibility entropy metrics, directly tying MoE-Harmony's core mixture-of-experts mechanism to biologically plausible correction modulation at cellular resolution.
analysis_perspective_015.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will exhibit a positive and stronger correlation between local batch effect strength and per-cell correction magnitude, indicating adaptive intensification of correction in regions with higher technical confounding and restraint in cleaner regions. Baseline Harmony will show a weaker or flatter relationship, reflecting a more global and less adaptive correction profile. This demonstrates MoE-Harmony's superior ability to differentiate and calibrate correction intensity in heterogeneous technical contexts, underpinning improved biological signal preservation while effectively reducing batch artifacts.
analysis_perspective_015.experimental_metric: Spearman correlation coefficient between local batch effect strength and per-cell correction magnitude
analysis_perspective_015.suitable_drawing_chart:
For the analysis perspective focusing on the correction magnitude scaling relative to local batch effect strength at the single-cell level, the most suitable visualization is a grouped scatter plot with a smooth trend overlay (e.g., loess curve) or box plot with binned local batch effect strength on the x-axis and correction magnitude on the y-axis for each method. However, since line charts are discouraged, a grouped violin plot or grouped box plot can better illustrate the distribution of per-cell correction magnitude across bins of local batch effect strength, for both MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony side-by-side. This allows visual comparison of the relationship and adaptive correction pattern, highlighting stronger correlation for MoE-Harmony and weaker one for baseline Harmony. 

Drawing plan:
- X-axis: Binned local batch effect strength (e.g., quantile bins)
- Y-axis: Correction magnitude (per-cell correction vector norm)
- Grouping/color: Integration method (MoE-Harmony vs Harmony)
- For each bin, show a violin or box plot of correction magnitudes
- Include statistical annotation for Spearman correlation coefficients in the plot legend or as text annotations

This chart type enables clear depiction of adaptive scaling patterns without relying on line charts and is aligned with visualization approaches used in similar single-cell integration benchmarking studies.

analysis_perspective_016.analysis_perspective: Localized Latent Manifold Preservation under Expert-Centric Correction
analysis_perspective_016.core_focus: Assess how MoE-Harmony preserves intrinsic single-cell latent manifold geometry and neighborhood structure during batch correction by leveraging expert-specific localized adjustments, compared to baseline Harmony's global correction which may distort manifold topology. This perspective evaluates the core advantage of MoE-Harmony's mixture-of-experts in maintaining biologically meaningful cellular relationships after integration, essential for downstream analyses like trajectory inference and cell state continuity.
analysis_perspective_016.differentiated_feature: This analysis introduces a latent manifold preservation metric computed by quantifying changes in local neighborhood graph structure and geodesic distances in the embedding space before and after batch correction. Specifically, it evaluates for each cell the consistency of k-nearest neighbor (k-NN) relationships and local manifold curvature by comparing neighborhood graph connectivity and shortest-path distances between original and corrected embeddings. The innovation lies in decomposing these preservation scores at the level of cells primarily assigned to individual experts, revealing how MoE-Harmony's localized expert corrections maintain local geometry better within expert domains than the baseline's uniform correction. Unlike existing analyses focusing on correction magnitude or subspace alignment, this approach directly quantifies topological and geometric fidelity of corrected embeddings, providing a novel quantitative link between MoE architecture and retention of biologically relevant manifold structure.
analysis_perspective_016.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will demonstrate significantly higher local neighborhood preservation scores and geodesic distance correlation within expert-assigned cell subsets, indicating superior retention of latent manifold geometry compared to baseline Harmony, which will show larger disruptions of neighborhood structure due to global, non-localized correction. This insight explains why MoE-Harmony better preserves biological cell state continuity and downstream trajectory or topology-dependent analyses, highlighting localized expert correction as a key driver of integration quality.
analysis_perspective_016.experimental_metric: Mean Local Neighborhood Preservation Score (e.g., average Jaccard index of k-NN sets between original and corrected embeddings) stratified by expert assignment
analysis_perspective_016.suitable_drawing_chart: A grouped violin plot or grouped box plot is most suitable to visualize the Local Neighborhood Preservation Scores stratified by expert assignments for both MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony methods. Each group on the x-axis represents cells assigned to a particular expert, with the preservation score distribution shown for MoE-Harmony and baseline side-by-side within each group. This allows clear visual comparison of local neighborhood preservation across methods per expert, highlighting MoE-Harmony's advantage in maintaining manifold geometry. The y-axis displays neighborhood preservation scores (e.g., Jaccard indices). This plot effectively conveys distributional differences, variation, and median preservation, supporting the analysis of localized expert-centric correction benefits.

analysis_perspective_017.analysis_perspective: Correction Influence Decay Stratification: Quantifying MoE-Harmony's Localized Correction Footprint Across Cellular Neighborhoods
analysis_perspective_017.core_focus: Evaluate how correction vectors applied by MoE-Harmony versus baseline Harmony propagate influence across cellular neighborhoods in high-dimensional single-cell embedding space, by stratifying cells based on their topological distance from the cells most strongly assigned to each expert. This analysis reveals whether MoE-Harmony's mixture-of-experts architecture confines batch effect correction influence to localized, expert-associated cellular neighborhoods, thereby preventing spurious correction spillover into unrelated biological regions and thus preserving biological heterogeneity more effectively.
analysis_perspective_017.differentiated_feature: This perspective introduces a novel neighborhood-based decay analysis: for each expert in MoE-Harmony, identify a focal set of cells with highest responsibility (strong assignment); then, calculate cellular correction magnitudes and directions as a function of graph-based geodesic distance (e.g., shortest-path or diffusion) from these focal cells in embedding space. Unlike global correction magnitude or responsibility entropy analyses, this method explicitly maps spatial decay profiles of expert-driven corrections to measure correction localization in neighborhood topology. The approach extends to baseline Harmony by artificially defining analogous focal cell sets (e.g., cells with largest global correction magnitude) to allow comparable decay characterization, revealing the difference in correction spread between methods. This granular, spatially-resolved correction footprint measurement operationalizes the core architectural premise of MoE-Harmony: localized expert corrections with constrained influence ranges.
analysis_perspective_017.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will demonstrate a sharp correction magnitude decay as cellular geodesic distance from expert 'core' cells increases, indicating highly localized correction footprint per expert concentrated in specific cellular neighborhoods. In contrast, baseline Harmony will exhibit flatter or more uniform correction influence across neighborhoods, reflecting global, non-localized corrections susceptible to overcorrecting distant biological states. This spatial correction influence decay profile explains MoE-Harmony’s fundamental advantage in selectively removing batch effects while minimizing unintended biological perturbation through spatially confined expert corrections.
analysis_perspective_017.experimental_metric: Correction magnitude decay slope with topological neighborhood distance from expert core cells (per expert for MoE-Harmony, global for baseline Harmony)
analysis_perspective_017.suitable_drawing_chart: Heatmap matrix showing correction magnitude decay versus topological neighborhood distance from expert core cells, with separate matrices for each expert in MoE-Harmony and a global decay matrix for baseline Harmony. Rows represent experts (MoE-Harmony) or method (Harmony), columns represent discrete distance bins from core cells, and color intensity encodes average correction magnitude at each distance. This visual stratification enables direct comparison of localized decay profiles across experts and between methods, highlighting the localized correction footprint in MoEHarmony versus the broader spread in baseline Harmony without relying on line charts.

analysis_perspective_018.analysis_perspective: Localized Correction Impact on Downstream Biological Signal Sensitivity in Rare Cell States
analysis_perspective_018.core_focus: Quantitatively evaluate how MoE-Harmony's localized, mixture-of-experts batch corrections differentially influence the sensitivity of downstream biological analyses (e.g., differential gene expression or cell state trajectory inference) in rare or intermediate cell states compared to baseline Harmony's uniform correction. This perspective reveals whether MoE-Harmony’s expert-wise corrections preserve subtle biological signals critical for interpreting rare cellular subpopulations and transitional states, which are often masked by global batch corrections.
analysis_perspective_018.differentiated_feature: This perspective uniquely links the localized correction vectors from MoE-Harmony to direct changes in downstream sensitivity metrics stratified by cell rarity or cell state intermediacy. The experimental design entails: (1) identifying rare or intermediate cell states via cell-type annotations or trajectory pseudotime; (2) separately quantifying correction magnitude and correction vector direction concordance within these states; (3) performing downstream analyses sensitive to biological variance (e.g., differential expression testing for marker genes, trajectory continuity or pseudotime inference quality) before and after integration; and (4) assessing the preservation or enhancement of biological signal sensitivity metrics stratified by cell rarity or state. Unlike global correction or subspace-alignment analyses, this method explicitly documents the functional impact of localized corrections on the ability to detect meaningful biology in challenging subpopulations, supporting the biological relevance of MoE-Harmony's core technical innovation.
analysis_perspective_018.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will show higher preservation of biological signal sensitivity in rare or intermediate cell states—reflected by stronger differential expression signals, more consistent trajectory inference, and less distortion of subtle biological features—compared to baseline Harmony, which is prone to overcorrect and obscure these signals due to a non-localized correction approach. This indicates that the mixture-of-experts architecture’s capacity to localize corrections prevents the erosion of critical biological information in rare or transitional populations, explaining improved outcomes in downstream analyses requiring nuanced biological fidelity.
analysis_perspective_018.experimental_metric: Change in differential expression effect size and significance (e.g., average log fold-change and adjusted p-value of marker genes) in rare/intermediate cell states after integration compared between MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony
analysis_perspective_018.suitable_drawing_chart:
Grouped Violin Plots or Boxplots comparing change metrics

- X-axis: Different methods (MoE-Harmony vs baseline Harmony)
- Grouped by cell rarity/state categories (Rare cells, Intermediate states, Common cells)
- Y-axis: Downstream sensitivity metrics (e.g., change in average log fold-change, significance p-values, or marker perturbation scores)

Chart details:
- Separate plots or facets showing differential expression effect size changes and significance changes.
- Overlaying data points for individual marker genes or cell states to show distribution.
- Statistical summary (median, quartiles) to highlight differences.

Rationale:
This chart allows clear visualization of how the localized corrections by MoE-Harmony affect marker gene sensitivity differently across rare/intermediate vs common cell states compared to baseline. Violin or boxplots effectively show distributional changes in quantitative effect size and statistical significance metrics, highlighting preservation or improvement of biological signals in challenging subpopulations.

analysis_perspective_019.analysis_perspective: Correction Magnitude Distribution Skew Relative to Expert Responsibility Sharpness
analysis_perspective_019.core_focus: Examine how MoE-Harmony’s correction magnitude distribution per cell relates to the sharpness (peakedness) of the expert responsibility vector, quantifying whether cells with sharply assigned responsibilities receive more focused, intense corrections compared to those with dispersed or ambiguous expert assignments. This analysis reveals a fundamental mechanism by which the mixture-of-experts architecture allocates correction intensity depending on the decisiveness of expert assignment, differentiating MoE-Harmony's adaptive correction paradigm from baseline Harmony's uniform correction approach.
analysis_perspective_019.differentiated_feature: This perspective jointly evaluates, for each cell, (a) the sharpness of the responsibility vector measured by a peakedness metric such as the maximum responsibility or inverse entropy, and (b) the magnitude of the correction vector (L2 norm). By plotting correction magnitude against responsibility sharpness and characterizing the distribution skewness of correction magnitudes stratified by sharpness bins, it uncovers whether MoE-Harmony applies stronger, more concentrated corrections exclusively when the cell’s responsibility is unequivocal. Baseline Harmony’s uniform correction, lacking expert routing, is expected to show minimal or no dependence of correction magnitude on such sharpness metrics. This novel joint-distribution approach explicitly connects MoE-Harmony’s core innovation of responsibility-driven correction intensity modulation to correction magnitude allocation patterns, a perspective not covered by existing analyses of correction magnitude or entropy correlations alone.
analysis_perspective_019.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will display a positively skewed correction magnitude distribution with higher correction intensities predominantly in cells exhibiting high responsibility sharpness (strong, confident expert assignment), while cells with low sharpness have broadly lower and more uniform correction magnitudes. Baseline Harmony will exhibit a flatter, less skewed distribution with negligible dependence of correction magnitude on responsibility sharpness. This pattern highlights how MoE-Harmony leverages expert responsibility decisiveness to adaptively apply correction intensity, reducing overcorrection and preserving biological heterogeneity in ambiguous cells, thus explaining its superior integration fidelity.
analysis_perspective_019.experimental_metric: Skewness of per-cell correction magnitude distributions stratified by responsibility sharpness bins
analysis_perspective_019.suitable_drawing_chart: A scatter plot combined with a marginal distribution histogram is most suitable: Plot each cell as a point with x-axis = responsibility sharpness metric (e.g., max responsibility or inverse entropy) and y-axis = correction magnitude (L2 norm). Color points by sharpness bins to reveal clusters. Overlay trend lines or smoothing curves per sharpness bin to show dependency patterns. Alongside, marginal histograms or violin plots for correction magnitude distributions stratified by sharpness bins illustrate skewness differences. This visual strategy jointly captures the correlation and distributional skewness, clearly differentiating MoE-Harmony and baseline Harmony behaviors. Diagram plan: X-axis labeled 'Responsibility Sharpness'; Y-axis labeled 'Correction Magnitude'; colored scatter points grouped by sharpness bins; marginal histograms/violin plots on the right/above to show skewness of correction magnitudes within each bin.

analysis_perspective_020.analysis_perspective: Correction Influence Decay Stratification: Quantifying MoE-Harmony's Localized Correction Footprint Across Cellular Neighborhoods
analysis_perspective_020.core_focus: Evaluate how correction vectors applied by MoE-Harmony versus baseline Harmony propagate influence across cellular neighborhoods in high-dimensional single-cell embedding space, by stratifying cells based on their topological distance from the cells most strongly assigned to each expert. This analysis reveals whether MoE-Harmony's mixture-of-experts architecture confines batch effect correction influence to localized, expert-associated cellular neighborhoods, thereby preventing spurious correction spillover into unrelated biological regions and thus preserving biological heterogeneity more effectively.
analysis_perspective_020.differentiated_feature: This perspective introduces a novel neighborhood-based decay analysis: for each expert in MoE-Harmony, identify a focal set of cells with highest responsibility (strong assignment); then, calculate cellular correction magnitudes and directions as a function of graph-based geodesic distance (e.g., shortest-path or diffusion) from these focal cells in embedding space. Unlike global correction magnitude or responsibility entropy analyses, this method explicitly maps spatial decay profiles of expert-driven corrections to measure correction localization in neighborhood topology. The approach extends to baseline Harmony by artificially defining analogous focal cell sets (e.g., cells with largest global correction magnitude) to allow comparable decay characterization, revealing the difference in correction spread between methods. This granular, spatially-resolved correction footprint measurement operationalizes the core architectural premise of MoE-Harmony: localized expert corrections with constrained influence ranges.
analysis_perspective_020.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will demonstrate a sharp correction magnitude decay as cellular geodesic distance from expert 'core' cells increases, indicating highly localized correction footprint per expert concentrated in specific cellular neighborhoods. In contrast, baseline Harmony will exhibit flatter or more uniform correction influence across neighborhoods, reflecting global, non-localized corrections susceptible to overcorrecting distant biological states. This spatial correction influence decay profile explains MoE-Harmony’s fundamental advantage in selectively removing batch effects while minimizing unintended biological perturbation through spatially confined expert corrections.
analysis_perspective_020.experimental_metric: Correction magnitude decay slope with topological neighborhood distance from expert core cells (per expert for MoE-Harmony, global for baseline Harmony)
analysis_perspective_020.suitable_drawing_chart: A grouped bar chart (or grouped histogram) stratified by topological neighborhood distance bins, showing mean correction magnitude for each expert (for MoE-Harmony) and a single global profile for baseline Harmony. X-axis: discrete bins of geodesic distance from expert focal cells (e.g., 0, 1, 2, ... neighborhoods). Y-axis: average correction magnitude. Each group corresponds to one expert for MoE-Harmony, with distinctly colored bars side-by-side per distance bin; baseline Harmony shown as a single color group for comparison. This bar chart visually conveys spatial decay and localization differences across experts without relying on line charts and highlights sparse vs. diffuse correction footprints prominently.

analysis_perspective_021.analysis_perspective: Covariate-Driven Expert Correction Decomposition: Disentangling Expert-Specific Correction Contributions to Complex Multi-Covariate Interactions
analysis_perspective_021.core_focus: To dissect and quantify how each expert within MoE-Harmony differentially responds to distinct combinations of multiple confounded technical and biological covariates, revealing the mechanistic basis of its localized correction advantage over baseline Harmony in handling complex covariate effects that are deeply intertwined.
analysis_perspective_021.differentiated_feature: This novel analysis uniquely decomposes the correction vectors at the per-expert level into constituent components attributable to individual technical and biological covariates and their interactions by leveraging regression or variance component analysis on the per-expert correction matrices projected along known covariate design bases. Unlike existing analyses focusing on overall expert responsibility, correction magnitude, or global batch effect removal, this approach operationalizes an explicit expert-by-covariate correction attribution matrix that reveals how each expert selectively targets specific covariate confounding patterns or their interactions, even when covariates are highly correlated or confounded. This fine-grained decomposition elucidates whether MoE-Harmony’s mixture-of-experts architecture effectively disentangles and locally resolves complex multi-covariate batch effects by assigning dedicated correction components to specialized experts, a perspective not addressed by prior global or single-covariate analyses and directly linked to the method's core innovation of localized, expert-specific correction modeling.
analysis_perspective_021.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will demonstrate clear, interpretable patterns where individual experts dominantly correct distinct covariate-associated variation components or their interactions, evidenced by high variance explained or effect sizes in corresponding covariate correction components per expert. In contrast, baseline Harmony, lacking expert modularity, will show diffuse correction contributions with less disentangled attribution, reflecting global corrections inadequately resolving complex confounding interactions. This expert-level covariate-driven correction decomposition explains the fundamental reason MoE-Harmony better preserves biological signals while removing multifactorial batch effects by localizing and disentangling confounded correction contributions within experts.
analysis_perspective_021.experimental_metric: Per-expert Covariate Correction Variance Explained Ratio (explained variance fraction of correction attributable to each technical and biological covariate per expert)
analysis_perspective_021.suitable_drawing_chart: A clustered heatmap is most suitable for visualizing the Per-expert Covariate Correction Variance Explained Ratio. Rows represent individual experts in the MoE-Harmony model, and columns represent each technical and biological covariate plus their interaction terms. The color intensity in each cell encodes the variance explained fraction by that expert for that covariate, providing a clear comparative visualization of expert-specific correction attributions. Dendrograms on rows and columns can be optional to reveal grouping patterns in experts or covariates. This heatmap facilitates an intuitive assessment of specialization and disentanglement of correction components in MoE-Harmony versus diffuse patterns in baseline Harmony.

analysis_perspective_022.analysis_perspective: Expert-specific Covariate Decomposition of Correction Effects under Multi-factor Confounding
analysis_perspective_022.core_focus: To dissect how each expert in MoE-Harmony individually attributes batch effect corrections to distinct, potentially confounded technical and biological covariates and their interactions, revealing the mechanistic basis for its superior batch effect removal and biological signal preservation compared to baseline Harmony's global correction approach.
analysis_perspective_022.differentiated_feature: This novel analytical perspective decomposes per-expert correction vectors into contributions attributable to individual covariates and their interactions by applying regression or variance component analysis of the per-expert correction matrices projected onto known covariate design spaces. Unlike prior analyses focusing on global correction magnitude or expert responsibilities, it explicitly characterizes the specialization and disentanglement of correction components at the expert level under complex multi-covariate confounding conditions, directly linked to MoE-Harmony’s architectural innovation of localized expert corrections.
analysis_perspective_022.expected_insight: MoE-Harmony will exhibit distinct, interpretable patterns where each expert dominantly corrects specific covariate-associated variation components or their interactions, with higher variance explained per covariate per expert, while baseline Harmony will show diffused and less disentangled correction attribution across covariates, reflecting an indiscriminate global correction. This validates the fundamental mechanism by which MoE-Harmony effectively isolates and removes complex batch effects while preserving biological complexity through expert-wise disentanglement.
analysis_perspective_022.experimental_metric: Per-expert Covariate Correction Variance Explained Ratio (fraction of correction variance explained by each known covariate and their interactions per expert)
analysis_perspective_022.suitable_drawing_chart: A clustered heatmap is the most suitable chart to visualize the per-expert Covariate Correction Variance Explained Ratio. The heatmap's rows correspond to the different experts in MoE-Harmony, and the columns represent individual known covariates and their interactions. The color intensity in each cell indicates the fraction of correction variance explained by a particular covariate-expert pair, thus revealing specialization and disentanglement patterns. Clustering both rows and columns will highlight groups of experts that dominantly correct similar covariate-associated variation components and reveal covariate relationships. This visual representation directly conveys the core insight of expert-specific correction disentanglement and allows straightforward comparison with baseline Harmony's global correction (which is expected to show a more uniform, less structured heatmap). Avoiding line charts mitigates clutter and better suits the multi-dimensional pattern discovery in this context.
